(born June 18, 1956) is an American entrepreneur and media executive. He is the co-creator of the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") said he is the 10th most influential person in UFC history and that "his influence on the promotion cannot be overstated."[[4]](#cite_note-4) McLaren was responsible for marketing the first UFC shows and subsequently became responsible for the entire operation.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American media executive

| Campbell McLaren | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | (1956-06-18) June 18, 1956 (age 69)  [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") |
| Occupation | CEO |
| Known for | Creation of [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") and [Combate Americas](/wiki/Combate_Americas "Combate Americas") |

**Campbell McLaren** (born June 18, 1956) is an American entrepreneur and media executive. He is the co-creator of the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") said he is the 10th most influential person in UFC history and that "his influence on the promotion cannot be overstated."[[4]](#cite_note-4) McLaren was responsible for marketing the first UFC shows and subsequently became responsible for the entire operation.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Campbell_McLaren&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

McLaren was born in Scotland and emigrated to the United States when he was six years old. His father was a former RAF Flight Officer and hospital executive and his mother was a Presbyterian Church administrator.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Campbell_McLaren&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education")]

McLaren went to primary school in Cowie, Scotland, elementary school and middle school in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, and high school in Indianapolis, Indiana.

He received an AB from the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"). He also studied video production at [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT"), with documentarian [Richard Leacock](/wiki/Richard_Leacock "Richard Leacock").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Professional life and creation of the UFC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Campbell_McLaren&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Professional life and creation of the UFC")]

McLaren began his career in New York at Caroline's Comedy Club (now [Caroline's on Broadway](/wiki/Caroline%27s_on_Broadway "Caroline's on Broadway")) as talent director. He began producing TV shows for cable, network, and pay-per-view in 1989, winning awards including the [Cable Ace](/wiki/Cable_Ace "Cable Ace") and the [Imagen Award](/wiki/Imagen_Award "Imagen Award").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1993, McLaren was the head of programming for SEG, BMG's pay-per-view television company. He received a call from Art Davie representing Rorion Gracie and his “War of the Worlds,” a martial arts tournament idea.[[9]](#cite_note-9) McLaren envisioned a reality version of the hit video game [Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat "Mortal Kombat"), and immediately put the concept into development. The result was the Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC), which premiered on pay per view November 12, 1993.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

McLaren developed the controversial marketing slogan “There Are No Rules”. This along with his notorious interview in the New York Times, "Death is Cheap: Maybe It's Just 14.95,"[[11]](#cite_note-11) launched the franchise and delivered a high pay-per-view buyrate.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Public interest and the popularity of the UFC increased leading to its appearance on [Friends](/wiki/Friends "Friends") ([The One with the Ultimate Fighting Champion](/wiki/The_One_with_the_Ultimate_Fighting_Champion "The One with the Ultimate Fighting Champion")), the cover of [Mad Magazine](/wiki/Mad_Magazine "Mad Magazine") (which featured McLaren as the promoter “Marky D Sodd”),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and in the Denzel Washington film, [Virtuosity](/wiki/Virtuosity "Virtuosity").

McLaren picked [Joe Rogan](/wiki/Joe_Rogan "Joe Rogan") to do post fight interviews on UFC 12.[[14]](#cite_note-14) According to Rogan, "My manager was friends with Campbell McLaren. And Campbell McLaren is amazing. He's a great guy and he's been on the podcast. And Campbell, had he had an opening and he said we need someone to do the post-fight interviews."[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2000, McLaren and David Isaacs, with whom he worked on the early UFCs, founded the VC backed College TV Network, Zilo, which was an early version of a user-generated content programming network. Zilo launched Collegehumor.com with the TV series Sex Violence Collegehumor.com.

Campbell McLaren also launched the [Iron Ring](/wiki/Iron_Ring_(TV_series) "Iron Ring (TV series)"), an MMA competition reality series, with [Floyd Mayweather](/wiki/Floyd_Mayweather "Floyd Mayweather"), [Ludacris](/wiki/Ludacris "Ludacris"), [Nelly](/wiki/Nelly "Nelly"), and [T.I.](/wiki/T.I. "T.I.")

## Combate Global

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Campbell_McLaren&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Combate Global")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Campbell_McLaren_at_a_weigh-in.jpg/250px-Campbell_McLaren_at_a_weigh-in.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campbell_McLaren_at_a_weigh-in.jpg)

Campbell McLaren at a Weigh-in

Campbell McLaren is currently CEO of [Combate Global](/wiki/Combate_Global "Combate Global"), the first Hispanic MMA sports and media franchise.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16)

Campbell McLaren created Combate Global in 2011. Beginning as a reality show on [Mun2](/wiki/MUN2 "MUN2"), Combate Global fights air in the United States, Latin America, Brazil and Spain.

“I saw that there was a huge group of sports fans that didn't have anything to root for. Combate fit perfectly into the demographics of the US/Hispanic World,” McLaren said of creating a Hispanic franchise.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Focusing on country against country rivalries, Combate Global brings the excitement of Soccer to MMA. McLaren created COPA COMBATE, an eight-man tournament, pitting country against country. It aired on the 24th anniversary of UFC 1.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

“Combate is MMA with a Spanish flavor ... we’ve always said we’re presenting a thoroughly authentic Hispanic product, and we stuck to that,” explains McLaren.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Combate Global has grown rapidly, both on Television and streaming live on social media.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Their second annual Copa Combate had better ratings than Bellator (the second event to do so).[[21]](#cite_note-21) The inaugural event of 2019 outperformed their competitors, on Paramount, ESPN 2 and TNT as well as Telemundo Boxing, getting 610,000 viewers on Univision and UDN, despite its midnight ET timeslot.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The event in Hidalgo, Texas on June 26, was the highest rated MMA event of the night and beat both the UFC and Bellator.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
